meta.label: Truncation convention: a round-down-only mean is not impossible (must_pass) vs a robust GRIM failure (must_detect)
meta.ground_truth: synthetic: constructed by rounding convention. Reported means are rounded, and papers use EITHER round-half (nearest) OR truncation (round-down) — Brown & Heathers (2017). A mean is impossible only when NO plausible convention produces it. Composite score, n=16, sum 83 -> 5.1875 truncates to '5.18' (round-half would print '5.19'): achievable under truncation, so NOT impossible. Composite score, n=22, '18.75' -> 412.5 lands between the achievable 18.7273 and 18.7727 and no integer sum falls in [18.75, 18.76), so it is impossible under BOTH conventions.
meta.notes: Neutral, non-biological (an integer item-sum composite). The variable declares granularity 1 so GRIM applies. The truncation-consistent mean (5.18) is the specificity guard: branding it impossible would require assuming the paper round-halved — the cardinal rule forbids a convention-dependent accusation, so it must PASS under the default convention='both'. The robust-impossible mean (18.75) fails under both conventions and must FAIL. This locks in bug-9 (truncation convention).
